seoTitle: How to Create a Gauge Chart in Power BI to Track Progress
metaDescription: Build a gauge chart in Power BI to visually track progress against a target, ideal for monitoring sales, project completion, and resource use.
intro: Seeing progress against a target at a glance is often more actionable than reading a raw number off a table. This guide walks through creating a gauge chart in Power BI, from initial setup through customization for visual impact. It's designed for business analysts and professionals who want a clear, immediate read on how performance compares to a goal.

steps.heading: Set up your gauge chart
steps.body: Add the Gauge visual to your report and connect it to the measure representing current progress, such as sales achieved so far.

steps.heading: Customize your gauge chart
steps.body: Define the minimum, maximum, and target values so the gauge accurately reflects the range and goal relevant to your metric.

steps.heading: Understand use cases for gauge charts
steps.body: Apply the gauge to scenarios like monitoring sales performance against a monthly quota, tracking project completion rates, or watching resource utilization.

steps.heading: Explore automation for data updates
steps.body: Connect the gauge to a data source that refreshes automatically, so the visual always reflects the most current progress without manual updates.

faq.question: What's the difference between the Gauge visual and a simple progress bar?
faq.answer: A gauge presents progress on a dial with a target marker, giving a more visual sense of range and goal, while a progress bar is a simpler linear representation.

faq.question: Can I use a gauge chart to track progress toward a deadline?
faq.answer: Yes, as long as you can express progress as a numeric value relative to a target, gauges work well for time-based or completion-based tracking.

faq.question: Why does my gauge chart look empty or show zero?
faq.answer: This is often caused by the value or target measure returning blank due to filters, or a mismatch between the minimum and maximum range settings.

faq.question: Can multiple team members' progress be shown on one gauge chart?
faq.answer: A single gauge shows one aggregated value, so comparing multiple people usually means creating a separate gauge for each, or using another visual better suited to comparisons.
